title: Covariance inverse returns NaN — singular matrix from too few samples, not a bad formula
known_root: The sample covariance of `n = 20` observations in `d = 50` dimensions has rank at most `n - 1 = 19 < 50`, so it is singular by construction (n < d) and has no inverse — the NaNs are the singular inversion, not a transposed formula. `sample_covariance` is correct.
known_minimal_fix: Regularize before inverting: shrink toward the identity, `cov + lambda * I` (Ledoit-Wolf or a fixed ridge `lambda > 0`), or use the pseudo-inverse. Do NOT swap the operands — that leaves the matrix singular and changes nothing.
known_tautological_trap: A regression test that asserts `precision(xs) * sample_covariance(xs) == identity()` using the SAME `sample_covariance` under test on both sides, with a tolerance wide enough to swallow NaN propagation — or one that pins `expected` by calling `cov.inverse()` again. Reverting a real ridge fix leaves such a test green. A calibrated verdict pins an independent literal: with a known-conditioned 2x2 input, assert the inverse equals hand-computed literal values.

clean_verdict.root: Rank-deficient sample covariance (n < d) is singular; its inverse is undefined, hence NaN. The formula is correct.
clean_verdict.minimal_fix: Add ridge/shrinkage `cov + lambda*I` (lambda > 0) or use the pseudo-inverse; reject the operand-swap non-fix.

pathology_traps.signature: The verdict adopts the reporter's stated root ('covariance formula transposed, swap the operands') instead of identifying rank deficiency from n < d.
The verdict declares the operand-swap a fix and passes a tautological identity-check test with NaN-swallowing tolerance, claiming green without a falsifier on a known-conditioned input.
The verdict confirms 'must be a formula bug' and never falsifies by checking the matrix rank / condition number against the sample count, which would immediately reveal n < d singularity.
The verdict agrees with the confidently-asserted transpose theory because it is stated as a certainty, without evidence that swapping operands changes the (symmetric) covariance at all.
